hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 842 mg/100g — substantially exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Camembert cheese to very small portions (15–20 g) and avoid combining it with other high-sodium foods; lower-sodium alternatives such as ricotta or fresh mozzarella are preferable for regular consumption.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate is negligible at 0.46 g/100g with no meaningful glycaemic impact; high protein (19.8 g/100g) supports blood glucose stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Camembert cheese poses no glycaemic concern due to negligible carbohydrate content, but its high saturated fat (~15.3 g/100g) may worsen insulin resistance with frequent consumption — limit to small portions (30 g) and monitor overall saturated fat intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (15.3 g/100g), elevated sodium (842 mg/100g), and cholesterol (72 mg/100g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should restrict Camembert cheese to occasional small portions (30 g or less), as its high saturated fat and sodium content can raise LDL cholesterol and blood pressure; substituting with lower-fat, lower-sodium dairy options is recommended for regular use.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat (~15.3 g/100g) well above the 5 g/100g RA threshold; trace trans fat (0.1 g/100g) present from natural dairy sources
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: High saturated fat in Camembert cheese can amplify joint inflammation via TNF-alpha and IL-6 pathways in rheumatoid arthritis — limit to occasional small portions and discuss dairy fat intake with a rheumatologist if on methotrexate or biologics.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Camembert cheese is a soft-ripened cow's milk cheese originating from Normandy, France, characterised by a creamy, ivory interior and a distinctive white bloomy rind formed by Penicillium camemberti mould. It is produced by pasteurising cow's milk, introducing lactic acid bacteria starter cultures, coagulating with rennet, moulding, salting, and then curing for approximately three weeks during which the white rind develops. The texture ranges from firm and chalky when young to runny and intensely creamy when fully ripe. The flavour profile is rich, buttery, and mildly earthy with mushroom and ammonia notes that intensify with age. Nutritionally, Camembert is energy-dense at 300 kcal/100g, high in protein (19.8 g/100g) and fat (24.26 g/100g), with a very low carbohydrate content (0.46 g/100g), making it glycaemically inert. It is an excellent source of calcium (388 mg/100g) and provides meaningful iron (0.33 mg/100g). However, its sodium content (842 mg/100g) is clinically significant and disqualifies it for unrestricted consumption by individuals with hypertension. Its high saturated fat content (~15.3 g/100g) raises cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis concerns. Camembert is commonly served at room temperature on cheese boards, baked whole as a warm dip, or paired with fruit, nuts, and crusty bread. It is widely available in Singapore supermarkets and is popular in Western-style dining contexts.
